title: Questions on the Philosophy of Evas last episodes
display_title: Questions on the Philosophy of Evas last episodes
wikitext:
[]: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by Astral Scarecrow » Thu May 05, 2016 12:11 pm Evangelion has always been like a home base for my life philosophy and motivation, but it's come to my attention that I don't fully understand some of its message. In one of the last episodes, there is a heavy emphasis on Shinji's realization that "others' perception of him forms his true self." This is framed as one of Eva's ultimate messages. What does this mean, and how does the show justify what it means?

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by Sachi » Thu May 05, 2016 12:33 pm Basically it means that the way others perceive him is just as valid as the way he perceives himself as well as the way he actually is. Nobody knows what's going on in your head, so they only have your actions to judge. Their picture of you may be incomplete, but it's not false. Which means, to an extent, we're responsible for our reputation. There is no "I" without others.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu May 05, 2016 4:11 pm A lot of Shinji's behaviour is reacting to his model of others, that they in turn model him as various flavours of useless. Part of the revelation when the barriers go down in the various case of whomever sections is that, hey, the Shinji in other peoples' minds isn't such a bad chap after all. The moral of the story is about not beating yourself up on what you think is other peoples' behalf.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Thu May 05, 2016 4:26 pm Sachi wrote: Basically it means that the way others perceive him is just as valid as the way he perceives himself as well as the way he actually is. Nobody knows what's going on in your head, so they only have your actions to judge. Their picture of you may be incomplete, but it's not false. Which means, to an extent, we're responsible for our reputation. There is no "I" without others.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by Sachi » Thu May 05, 2016 9:59 pm xanderkh wrote: Hopefully, this only means to those that are close to you, instead of being worried about the version of "you" seen by strangers, cause you can't always control what people think of you, no matter what you do. In a sense, that person perceived as you that exists in the mind of others is a separate, yet valid entity, it's still independent of you, and beyond your control. Therefore, if it's beyond your control, why worry about the perception of others too much? Not saying that we shouldn't be worried about our reputation, but you shouldn't let it define you as a person what other's think and/or expect of you. Be you, and live the way YOU want to. But most importantly, be happy with who YOU are, not what others think of you.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri May 06, 2016 12:50 am For every charecter and their interactions with another there's a whole host of issues to ponder: who A actually is who A thinks A is who A thinks B is who A thinks B thinks that A is who B thinks A thinks that B is who B thinks A is who B thinks B is who B actually is

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by Astral Scarecrow » Fri May 06, 2016 1:13 pm Yeah, the thing is, I believe that Eva makes the case that "who B thinks A is" = "who A actually is," with "who A thinks A is" being discarded. This is what Shinji states and is reaffirmed by the versions of the other characters in his head. I don't necessarily agree with this statement, or understand it, like what is it that makes other people's opinion of you "true?" As a result of this confusion I feel there must be more to it. In response to Sachi, I think you're right that Eva makes the case that others' interpretation of your actions is valid, but in reality, everyone views each other with a certain tone; your friends might view you as giving off an entirely different tone or even being a different person, even if your actions toward all of them are the same. My point is that people's minds create an emotional mental image attached to you. I can completely understand why these various perceptions would form (to quote the Leliel episode) the Shinji Ikari in Gendo's mind, the Shinji Ikari in Rei's mind, etc, as long as they are all different Shinji Ikaris. This is what episode 16 implies, but the last episodes make the case that those individual perceptions are what form the "true" Shinji Ikari, instead of being perceptions of multiple Shinji Ikaris. I hope this made sense and that you can understand my distinction between the two arguments.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri May 06, 2016 2:30 pm Astral Scarecrow : Episode 16 provides a rather clear thesis. For everyone's reference: Shinji B (OFF): I am you. People have another self within themselves. The self is always composed of two people. Shinji A (OFF): Two people? Shinji B (OFF): The self which is actually seen, and the self observing that. There are many entities called Shinji Ikari. The other Shinji Ikari that exists in your mind. The Shinji Ikari in Misato Katsuragi's mind, the Shinji in Asuka Sohryu, the Shinji in Rei Ayanami, and the Shinji in Gendo Ikari. All are different Shinji Ikaris, but each of them is a true Shinji Ikari.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri May 06, 2016 7:12 pm It's basically a "death of the author" argument regarding correct reading of a text. In this case the individual person themselves being the text, and thus their view of themselves is no more privileged than anyone else's opinion. All such views have lasting effects in both thought and action for all involved, so even if mistaken they are still meaningful and thus real.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by Sachi » Sat May 07, 2016 2:29 am Astral Scarecrow wrote: In response to Sachi, I think you're right that Eva makes the case that others' interpretation of your actions is valid, but in reality, everyone views each other with a certain tone; your friends might view you as giving off an entirely different tone or even being a different person, even if your actions toward all of them are the same. My point is that people's minds create an emotional mental image attached to you. I can completely understand why these various perceptions would form (to quote the Leliel episode) the Shinji Ikari in Gendo's mind, the Shinji Ikari in Rei's mind, etc, as long as they are all different Shinji Ikaris. This is what episode 16 implies, but the last episodes make the case that those individual perceptions are what form the "true" Shinji Ikari, instead of being perceptions of multiple Shinji Ikaris. I hope this made sense and that you can understand my distinction between the two arguments.

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by sephirotic » Mon May 09, 2016 4:58 pm The development of identity is a key recurring theme with many psychoanalyst. Eva is known to have psychoanalitical theories from at least Freud, Klein and Jung (meh) embended in its narration, the final EoTV episode strikes strongly to me as Klein subject-object theory, I'll just throw this here: http://changingminds.org/disciplines/ps ... /klein.htm

[]: Re: Questions on the Philosophy of Eva's last episodes             Quote Post by zlink64 » Mon May 16, 2016 8:17 pm Astral Scarecrow wrote: Yeah, the thing is, I believe that Eva makes the case that "who B thinks A is" = "who A actually is," with "who A thinks A is" being discarded. This is what Shinji states and is reaffirmed by the versions of the other characters in his head. I don't necessarily agree with this statement, or understand it, like what is it that makes other people's opinion of you "true?" As a result of this confusion I feel there must be more to it.

This is what episode 16 implies, but the last episodes make the case that those individual perceptions are what form the "true" Shinji Ikari, instead of being perceptions of multiple Shinji Ikaris. I hope this made sense and that you can understand my distinction between the two arguments.
